The two are negatively correlated and label-complementary: the gap supplies refusal-specificity and the weight energy supplies recall. On a 273-checkpoint registry spanning Qwen, DeepSeek-distilled Qwen, Llama, and Gemma, their z-sum separates 57 public abliterations from 37 benign fine-tunes, merges, and instruction-tunes at AUROC 0.95, significantly above either signal alone (0.84, 0.90), and a Youden-calibrated threshold transfers to held-out families at balanced accuracy 0.89 (FPR 0.11), missing only 4 of 57. We then map two failures, in order of severity: a spoofed reference evades both axes with no training (ΔW=0, \r{ho}=1 by construction), and a white-box owner trains a checkpoint past the threshold while it stays guard-unsafe and coherent. The audit is effective triage, not tamper-proofing: it presumes an attested reference, and its claims are bounded by the registry we evaluate it on.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a threshold-free method to audit open-weight checkpoints for stripped refusal mechanisms before deployment.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to address the inability of runtime guards to assess whether an open-weight checkpoint lacks refusal mechanisms, focusing instead on auditing the artifact itself before deployment.
AI.method: Two internal signals are combined: (1) a reference-anchored activation refusal-gap and (2) a weight-recovery energy calculated from the difference between base and candidate weights. These signals are negatively correlated and complement each other in labeling.
AI.result: The proposed audit achieves an AUROC of 0.95, outperforming individual signals. It identifies 57 abliterations and accurately distinguishes them from benign cases with a balanced accuracy of 0.89 in cross-family validation.
AI.conclusion: While the audit is effective for checkpoint triage, it has limitations such as dependency on an attested reference and vulnerability to spoofed references or checkpoint retraining by malicious actors.

title: Low-Latency Task-Oriented Image Transmission with Opportunistic Spectrum Access
comment: This work has been accepted for presentation at IEEE SPAWC 2026
summary: Communication systems designed for reliable data reconstruction, rather than task-oriented communication, typically rely on separate source and channel coding and incur high latency under limited spectrum availability and fading channels. To address this, we propose a transmission framework with opportunistic spectrum access, in which the transmitter sends discrete latent representations learned via a vector-quantized variational autoencoder (VQ-VAE) over idle licensed channels using standard digital modulation. The AI-powered receiver is still able to reconstruct task-related information from the heavily compressed data. We develop a cross-layer latency model that accounts for compression, block errors, retransmissions, and stochastic channel access. Results on latency-accuracy trade-offs show that the proposed scheme achieves at least 79- and 3.3-fold latency reductions with only 5.7% and 2.4% drops in classification accuracy compared to benchmarks using conventional source and channel coding. The framework enables low-latency communication and reliable task execution even under limited spectrum availability and challenging channel conditions.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a low-latency communication framework leveraging VQ-VAE and opportunistic spectrum access to reduce latency without significantly compromising task accuracy.
AI.motivation: Traditional communication systems rely on separate source/channel coding, resulting in high latency under spectrum limitations and fading channels.
AI.method: The proposed approach uses a vector-quantized variational autoencoder (VQ-VAE) to transmit compressed latent data via digital modulation over idle licensed channels, coupled with a cross-layer latency model.
AI.result: The framework achieves 79- and 3.3-fold latency reductions with only minimal drops in classification accuracy (5.7% and 2.4%) compared to conventional benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The study's approach enables efficient and reliable low-latency task-oriented communication, even in challenging environments with limited spectrum availability.

title: CausalSteward: An Agentic Divide-Conquer-Combine Copilot for Causal Discovery
summary: Learning causal models from high-dimensional data is a significant challenge, particularly in real-world settings where violations of core assumptions lead to causal identifiability issues. Although massive amounts of prior knowledge are available, and contain valuable causal information, effectively integrating this knowledge into the causal discovery process remains an open problem. We introduce CausalSTeward (CAST), a novel human-in-the-loop framework for interactively assembling large causal models. CausalSteward is a multi-agent collaborative system that tackles high-dimensional causality through a divide-and-conquer approach where large clusters of variables are iteratively partitioned and then separately analyzed. Our framework fuses prior knowledge with a data-driven approach by using tailored tools such as retrieval augmented generation and conditional independence tests. Finally, we use this work to examine the capabilities and limitations of causal reasoning in multi-agent frameworks, and how the human-in-the-loop can contribute to accurate and trustworthy results.

AI.tldr: CausalSTeward (CAST) is a system that integrates human collaboration and prior knowledge to address challenges in high-dimensional causal model learning, using a divide-and-conquer approach combined with data-driven tools.
AI.motivation: The need arises due to challenges in causal model learning from high-dimensional data, especially when real-world settings disrupt core assumptions and cause identifiability problems.
AI.method: CausalSTeward employs a multi-agent collaborative system to partition variable clusters iteratively, integrating human input and prior knowledge with tools like retrieval augmented generation and conditional independence tests.
AI.result: CAST demonstrates the ability to systematically analyze large causal structures and highlights the contributions of human intervention in improving causal reasoning.
AI.conclusion: The framework underscores the importance of integrating prior knowledge and human involvement for accurate and trustworthy causal discovery results in complex, high-dimensional scenarios.

title: Bringing Agentic Search to Earth Observation Data Discovery
summary: NASA and its data centers hold thousands of geoscience datasets and tools like Worldview, Giovanni, the Science Discovery Engine, and Harmony. Finding the right one is hard even for domain experts. We present an agentic search system, deployed as a public service for the geoscience community, that takes a natural-language research query and returns the matching datasets and tools. We demonstrate that, in the era of large language models, the latent value of knowledge graphs (KGs) can be substantially amplified through agentic search. From the NASA Earth Observation Knowledge Graph (NASA EO-KG) we derive NASA-EO-Bench, an open benchmark of 47k query-dataset pairs (21k task-based queries). A neural scorer fine-tuned on NASA-EO-Bench beats cosine and BM25 baselines. Further combining it with BM25 via score fusion raises both Recall@10 (R@10) and MRR by over 5x. On top of this supervised pipeline, we add a zero-shot agentic reranking stage that, without any additional training, lifts MRR by 28% on a stratified N=200 subset, showing that LLM reasoning is complementary to supervised retrieval.

AI.tldr: This paper discusses a novel search system for geoscience datasets and tools that uses natural-language input, combining KGs and large language models to enhance retrieval performance.
AI.motivation: Finding the correct geoscience datasets and tools within NASA's vast repositories is a complex task, even for experts.
AI.method: The system integrates NASA's EO-KG, a new benchmark dataset (NASA-EO-Bench), and a neural scorer fine-tuned on these benchmarks. Further, it incorporates a zero-shot agentic reranking stage using LLMs.
AI.result: The proposed system outperforms baseline methods, achieving significant improvement in metrics like R@10 and MRR, while also benefiting from LLM-based reranking.
AI.conclusion: This work demonstrates that combining supervised retrieval methods with zero-shot reasoning in LLMs significantly improves search accuracy for geoscience datasets.

title: Q-GAIN: A Python Package for Machine Learning and Physically Informed Analysis Applications
comment: Submission to SciPost, 20 pages with 4 figures
summary: Here we describe the quantum gas analysis and inference (Q-GAIN) Python package, which enables rapid deployment of machine learning (ML) and physics-informed analysis techniques for cold-atom experiments. Out of the box, Q-GAIN implements classification, object detection, and physics-informed metrics for feature detection in images of atomic Bose-Einstein condensates (BECs). Q-GAIN encourages a natural, module-based workflow: starting with data loading and preprocessing, followed by ML-based feature identification, and ending with conventional analysis techniques. We demonstrate this modularity by configuring Q-GAIN for three ML tasks. First, we demonstrate the basic workflow of the Q-GAIN framework by implementing the standard task of classifying handwritten digits from the MNIST dataset. Then, we re-implement our earlier soliton detection (SolDet) package in the Q-GAIN framework, enabling the detection and analysis of solitonic excitations in time-of-flight data. Finally, we develop an object-detection tool that identifies quantized vortices in images of ring-shaped BECs.

AI.tldr: The Q-GAIN Python package is introduced for machine learning and physics-informed analysis in cold-atom experiments. It provides classification, object detection, and physics-informed metrics for analyzing Bose-Einstein condensate images.
AI.motivation: To facilitate and accelerate the deployment of machine learning and physics-informed methodologies in cold-atom experimental research, especially for Bose-Einstein condensate analysis.
AI.method: Q-GAIN employs a modular design, comprising data loading, ML-based feature identification, and conventional analysis. It implements ML tasks such as classification, object detection, and feature detection.
AI.result: Q-GAIN was applied to three tasks: (1) Handwritten digit classification using MNIST data, (2) solitonic excitations detection and analysis re-using the SolDet package, and (3) object detection tools for identifying quantized vortices in ring-shaped BEC images.
AI.conclusion: Q-GAIN provides a flexible and modular tool for researchers to incorporate machine learning and physics-informed analysis into their workflows for cold-atom experimental studies.

title: A Multi-Branch Hierarchy-Aware Framework for Heterogeneous Audio Classification
summary: This technical report describes our system for Task 1 of the DCASE 2026 Challenge, which aims to classify heterogeneous audio recordings according to the Broad Sound Taxonomy (BST). The task requires both accurate second-level prediction and consistency with the top-level taxonomy. Our system is built on CLAP-based audio-text representations and is improved along three strategies: expanding the training set with a filtered subset of BSD35k, enhancing acoustic modeling with feature-specific branches, and refining predictions using hierarchy-aware classifiers and KNN-based post-processing. Among the acoustic features considered, the log-STFT branch provides the strongest single-model performance. With KNN-based post-processing, our best single system achieves a hierarchical F1 score (Hier. F1) of 80.84% on the BSD10k-v1.2 set under the same evaluation protocol as the baseline. We further construct ensemble systems by combining models with complementary acoustic features and classification heads, achieving Hier. F1 scores of 81.25% and 81.18%, respectively.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a high-performing audio classification system for the DCASE 2026 Challenge using CLAP-based representations, hierarchical classifiers, and an ensemble approach, achieving up to 81.25% Hierarchical F1 scores.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to accurately classify diverse audio recordings according to the Broad Sound Taxonomy while ensuring consistency across hierarchical levels.
AI.method: The system uses CLAP-based audio-text features and incorporates strategies like data expansion, feature-specific branches, hierarchy-aware classifiers, and KNN-based post-processing. Ensemble models with complementary features are also employed.
AI.result: The best single system achieves a Hier. F1 score of 80.84%, while ensemble systems achieve 81.25% and 81.18% on the BSD10k-v1.2 dataset, outperforming the baseline.
AI.conclusion: This study demonstrates that leveraging diverse strategies, hierarchy-aware methods, and ensemble models can significantly improve audio taxonomy classification performance.

title: Optimal Stabilizer Testing and Learning with Limited Quantum Memory
summary:
We study stabilizer state testing and learning with limited coherent quantum memory. Here an algorithm sequentially receives copies of an unknown $n$-qubit state, but may keep only $k$ qubits of coherent quantum memory between measurements. With unrestricted memory, seminal work of Gross, Nezami and Walter showed how to test $n$-qubit stabilizer states using $6$ copies, which is dimension independent, unlike the learning complexity of $Θ(n)$. We show that this testing-vs-learning separation is lost under memory constraints. More concretely we show that
  (1) The sample complexity of testing stabilizer states in the $k$-qubit memory framework is $Θ(n-k)$. Our upper bound goes via a novel connection to the hidden shift problem and the lower bound is proven using a novel approach to average case bounds on likelihood ratios via combinatorics of the stochastic orthogonal group.
  (2) The sample complexity of learning stabilizer states with $k$ qubits of memory, in the non-adaptive framework, is $Θ(n^2/k)$.
  As a further application of our techniques, we prove an exponential lower bound for purity testing even when the memory may be left coherent throughout the protocol. Our main results identify coherent quantum memory as the resource enabling the usual separation between stabilizer testing and learning. In particular, even with $k=0.99n$ qubits of memory, there is no constant-copy stabilizer tester; furthermore for $k=cn$ qubits of memory (for $0< c < 1$), stabilizer testing is as hard as learning, with both requiring $Θ(n)$ copies.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates stabilizer state testing and learning under constraints of limited coherent quantum memory. It identifies the memory constraints under which the usual separation between stabilizer state testing and learning complexities is lost.
AI.motivation: To explore how the constraints of limited coherent quantum memory affect the sample complexity of testing and learning stabilizer states, and whether there is a separation between these two tasks under such constraints.
AI.method: The study analyzes the $k$-qubit memory framework using novel connections to the hidden shift problem and introduces combinatorial techniques with the stochastic orthogonal group for lower bounds.
AI.result: Sample complexity for stabilizer state testing is $Θ(n-k)$, while state learning complexity is $Θ(n^2/k)$ under non-adaptive frameworks. Coherent quantum memory is identified as critical for the separation in testing and learning. An exponential lower bound for purity testing is also established.
AI.conclusion: Limited quantum memory removes the usual separations between stabilizer state testing and learning. Increasing memory capacity facilitates testing, while restricted memory capacity equates the difficulties of testing and learning stabilizer states.

title: An Efficient vLLM-Based Inference Pipeline for Unified Audio Understanding and Generation
summary: While Large Multimodal Models excel in comprehension, high-throughput inference engines lack native support for multimodal generation. This is severe in Speech Language Models, where generating multi-layered audio tokens via decoupled AR+NAR or synchronous Multi-Token Prediction (MTP) with delay-pattern interleaving conflicts with standard single-stream loops. We present a vLLM-based inference pipeline for unified speech understanding and generation. We extend autoregressive decoding to natively execute delay-pattern de-interleaving and coordinated multi-stream sampling, integrating an on-GPU acoustic decoder for end-to-end waveform synthesis. Crucially, we overcome the shared intuition that Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG) halves throughput. By co-scheduling paired conditional and unconditional requests within a continuous batch, our CFG implementation sustains 80% of non-CFG throughput, absorbing dual-request and logit merging overheads. We open-source our framework.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a vLLM-based inference pipeline for speech understanding and generation, optimizing throughput in multimodal models.
AI.motivation: High-throughput inference engines struggle with native support for multimodal tasks such as speech generation, primarily due to conflicts with standard processing loops and inefficiencies in handling synchronous tasks.
AI.method: The authors extend autoregressive decoding with mechanisms like delay-pattern de-interleaving and coordinated multi-stream sampling, integrate on-GPU acoustic decoding, and redesign CFG to sustain 80% throughput using a co-scheduling strategy.
AI.result: The proposed method significantly improves throughput for multimodal speech models while addressing conflicts with traditional single-stream inference systems.
AI.conclusion: The newly introduced inference pipeline demonstrates a scalable solution for efficient and unified speech generation and analysis, and the framework is made available as open-source.

title: Behind the Refusal: Determining Guardrail Activation via Behavioral Monitoring
summary: As Large Language Models (LLMs) and agentic systems become integrated into real-world applications, ensuring their safety and security is critical. Guardrail systems that detect and block malicious instructions sent to and from an LLM are an essential component of AI security. However, researchers conducting black-box adversarial emulation against production AI systems often struggle to determine whether a guardrail block or an LLM rejection has occurred. This distinction is important because the techniques used to bypass guardrails can differ substantially from those used to bypass LLM safety alignment, and has a material impact on attack technique selection and optimization. We propose the first black-box guardrail reconnaissance methodology, which detects the presence of a guardrail within a target AI system through behavioral monitoring of HTTP, lexical, and timing signals, assuming only black-box access and zero prior knowledge of the guardrail or AI system. Experiments demonstrate that our approach detects guardrail presence with 100% accuracy, with statistically significant behavioral separation between benign and malicious interactions (q < 0.001). Our approach further identifies the content categories a guardrail is designed to block, and distinguishes guardrail blocks from LLM rejection on unseen prompts with an average F1 score of 98%.

AI.tldr: Proposes a novel black-box methodology to detect guardrails in AI systems ensuring security and achieving high detection accuracy.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of distinguishing guardrail blocks from LLM rejections in AI systems, which has implications for attack method optimization.
AI.method: Introduced a black-box reconnaissance method using behavioral monitoring of HTTP, lexical, and timing signals to identify guardrails without prior knowledge.
AI.result: Achieved 100% accuracy in guardrail detection and 98% F1 score in distinguishing guardrail blocks from LLM rejections.
AI.conclusion: The methodology provides a robust and effective tool for detecting guardrails, enhancing adversarial emulation research and AI system transparency.

title: Overview of Risk Assessment and Management for Intelligent Systems under the AI Act and Beyond
comment: 6 pages, 1 figure, 1 table. Accepted at the IEEE International Carnahan Conference on Security Technology (ICCST 2026), October 14, 2026
summary: The society and emerging risk-based regulatory frameworks for AI underscore the need for rigorous risk assessment to ensure safe and reliable AI systems. In response to this imperative, this paper presents an overview of AI risk assessment (identification and analysis) and management methodologies. It begins by reviewing the worldwide regulatory landscape that drives the need for systematic AI risk assessment. Then we characterize the spectrum of AI-related risks identified in the literature, from technical failures to ethical and social impacts. Subsequently, it reviews key risk assessment methodologies proposed for AI systems, focusing on general frameworks. The paper highlights best practices and illuminates methodological gaps, highlighting areas for further research on AI risk assessment.

AI.tldr: This paper reviews global regulatory needs, risks, and methods for AI risk assessment and management.
AI.motivation: To address the increasing societal and regulatory demands for rigorous AI risk assessment and management.
AI.method: The paper reviews global regulatory frameworks, catalogs AI-related risks, and evaluates existing risk assessment methodologies, highlighting best practices and gaps.
AI.result: The research offers a structured overview of AI-related risks, current methodologies, and areas requiring further examination for risk management.
AI.conclusion: Systematic AI risk assessment is imperative, necessitating further research and method development to address existing gaps and ensure safe, reliable AI systems.

title: What Types of Human-AI Teams Exist?
summary: Human-AI teaming has received increasing attention in the literature. However, the range of studies conducted in multiple domains make it difficult to understand what types of teams are being studied, and in what ways are they similar/different from one another. In this study, we analyse 53 papers on human-AI teams and categorise them into five main clusters based on psychological taxonomies of teaming; AI Assistant, Ad-hoc Dependency, Ad-hoc Forced Dependency, Paired Equanimity, and Group Equanimity. Each cluster represents a unique combination of holistic team-level characteristics, indicating there are multiple disparate team types studied under the same definition. In turn, this raises the question of whether insights are truly transferable between papers. We conclude with guidance on how to identify the types of human-AI teams studied, a checklist for reporting a human-AI team in research work, and ways in which the field can be further synthesised.

AI.tldr: The study analyzed 53 papers on human-AI teams, categorizing them into five clusters based on psychological taxonomies to address their diverse definitions.
AI.motivation: The lack of clarity in understanding the diversity and similarities of human-AI teams across studies that make cross-paper insights difficult to transfer.
AI.method: Researchers analyzed 53 papers and classified them into five clusters: AI Assistant, Ad-hoc Dependency, Ad-hoc Forced Dependency, Paired Equanimity, and Group Equanimity.
AI.result: The categorization showed a broad variation in team types studied, indicating disparate team models under the same human-AI team definition.
AI.conclusion: Guidelines and checklists were proposed for reporting human-AI teams in research along with suggestions to synthesize the field for more transferable insights.

title: The Eticas AI Risk Taxonomy: Open Infrastructure for Operationalizing AI Audits
summary: The rapid deployment of AI systems across high-stakes domains has created urgent demand for standardized evaluation, yet the field remains fragmented across competing risk taxonomies that catalog risks without showing how an audit is executed. At least 74 AI risk taxonomies exist, and almost all stop at the catalog. The hard part of auditing is not naming a risk but operationalizing it: turning it into a test run against a real system, a measured value, a calibrated severity, and a defensible grade. This paper leads with that bridge. We present the operationalization layer Eticas has built and run, shown end to end on a single risk (PII leakage) against a public benchmark, and then the open taxonomy that makes the method scale. On GPT-4-0314, a disclosure risk that seven external frameworks require be controlled is measured at 0%, 51%, and 84% disclosure as adversarial conditioning increases, mapping through calibrated severity bands to a subcategory grade of E with a SYSTEMIC pattern. Around this example, the Eticas AI Risk Taxonomy v2.0.0 organizes 76 active subcategories across 10 categories and 20 sub-groups, with mappings to 18 external frameworks across compliance, reference, and academic tiers. Its category and sub-group layer is published under CC BY 4.0 as open semantic infrastructure with stable URIs and SKOS/JSON-LD distributions, and a worked subcategory example shows the operational layer down to its severity thresholds. The contribution is the demonstrated bridge from concept to graded finding, anchored by a clean separation of risks from the mechanisms by which they surface, and framed by an open-core model in which the conceptual scaffold is open and the methodology calibration is the practitioner layer. This is the infrastructure the AI auditing field needs: shared, open, and demonstrably operable.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses the fragmentation in AI risk evaluation, proposing a structured audit methodology and introducing the Eticas AI Risk Taxonomy v2.0.0 to bridge theory and practice.
AI.motivation: To tackle the fragmented approach to AI risk evaluation and auditing processes, focusing on operationalizing risk assessments effectively.
AI.method: The paper demonstrates operationalizing a risk (PII leakage) using Eticas’ methodology, providing calibrated grades and using the Eticas AI Risk Taxonomy v2.0.0.
AI.result: Risk (PII leakage) was measured on GPT-4 at different adversarial conditioning levels, yielding disclosure levels of 0%, 51%, and 84%. Results were mapped to severity bands, producing demonstrable findings and systemic risk categorizations.
AI.conclusion: The paper presents a scalable, standardized approach to AI risk auditing with an open taxonomy and operable methodology that addresses current gaps in the industry.

title: SelectTSL: Prompt-Guided Selective Target Sound Localization in Complex Scenarios
summary: Humans can selectively attend to a target sound and estimate its direction in complex scenarios, whereas such selective localization remains challenging for current deep learning-based systems. Sound source localization (SSL) has achieved remarkable success with deep learning, yet most methods localize all active sources without selectivity. Conversely, target sound extraction (TSE) extracts sources using multimodal prompts but typically fails to preserve the multichannel spatial information required for accurate localization. To bridge this gap, we formulate the task of prompt-guided selective target sound localization and propose SelectTSL, an end-to-end architecture that localizes only the user-specified target in multi-source acoustic scenes. Specifically, we design a target-aware selective localization strategy that employs a Prompt-Guided Selective Attention Module (PGSA) to generate prompt-informed embeddings. These embeddings guide an inter-channel phase difference (IPD) enhancer to refine raw phase cues, fusing with target magnitudes to jointly estimate direction of arrival (DoA) and target-source cardinality, i.e., the number of target sound sources. This coupled design effectively focuses on the user-specified target spatial cues for selective localization and also handles time-varying numbers of target sources. Extensive experiments on both synthetic data and real-world recordings demonstrate that our proposed method consistently outperforms other baselines and exhibits robust generalization to real acoustic environments.

AI.tldr: This paper presents SelectTSL, a deep learning system for selectively localizing user-specified sound sources in multi-source scenarios using a novel Prompt-Guided Selective Attention Module (PGSA).
AI.motivation: Current deep learning systems struggle with selectively localizing target sounds in complex multi-source environments, unlike humans who excel at this task. Most existing methods lack the capability to focus on user-specified targets while maintaining spatial accuracy.
AI.method: The paper introduces SelectTSL, an end-to-end selective sound localization system. It uses the PGSA module to produce prompt-informed embeddings and an inter-channel phase difference (IPD) enhancer to refine spatial cues, jointly estimating the direction of arrival (DoA) and target-source cardinality.
AI.result: SelectTSL significantly outperforms baseline methods on both synthetic and real-world datasets, showing robust generalization across various acoustic environments.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method successfully bridges the gap between sound source localization and target sound extraction by selectively localizing user-specified sound sources while maintaining spatial information and handling dynamic target counts.

title: Human Capital, Not Model Benchmarks, Predicts Hybrid Intelligence in Forecasting
summary: Whether pairing people with AI helps or hurts is usually reported as a single average effect. Using a real-money prediction market (Polymarket) as an objective, externally resolved benchmark, this pilot shows that the value of human-AI collaboration depends on a specific, measurable form of human capital. Analyzed at the level of the individual forecaster, hybrid performance is trimodal: most people either deferred to the model (matching it) or used it to rubber-stamp a prior guess (performing worse than the model alone), while a minority engaged in genuine complementary reasoning and reached accuracy matching or even exceeding (i.e., lower error than) the market itself. Collaborative traits (perspective-taking, intellectual humility, and curiosity) rather than raw cognitive ability or model benchmarks, distinguished who reached that mode. The results are preliminary but statistically robust, and motivate a pre-registered replication now in preparation.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates how human-AI collaboration performs in prediction markets, finding that its effectiveness is highly individual-dependent and influenced by collaborative traits.
AI.motivation: To understand the conditions under which human-AI collaboration can enhance performance and the key human traits that contribute to effective hybrid reasoning in objective tasks like prediction markets.
AI.method: The study analyzed individual forecaster performance on the Polymarket prediction platform, examining hybrid human-AI results and correlating them with collaborative and cognitive traits.
AI.result: Hybrid performance was trimodal: individuals either matched the AI, underperformed relative to the AI, or outperformed both the AI and the market. Collaborative traits like perspective-taking and intellectual humility, rather than cognitive ability, were key to outperforming benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Effective human-AI collaboration depends on specific human traits, not just cognitive skill or model capability. These findings suggest targeted ways to improve hybrid team performance through fostering specific collaborative qualities.
